text: HIT prescription involves manipulation of at least nine variables: work interval intensity, work interval duration, relief interval intensity, relief interval duration, exercise modality, number of repetitions, number of series, between-series recovery duration, and between-series recovery intensity.

HIT formats that elicit similar maximal cardiorespiratory responses can produce distinctly different anaerobic energy contributions, meaning cardiopulmonary response alone is insufficient for programming HIT.
Effective HIT requires exercise intensity at or above 95% of the minimal velocity or power that elicits VO2max (vVO2max) up to 100% of maximal sprinting speed.
Runners can accumulate up to 6-8 km at vVO2max per HIT session, representing substantial neuromuscular and musculoskeletal strain.
Anaerobic glycolytic energy contribution and neuromuscular load should be considered alongside cardiopulmonary responses when programming HIT to maximize training outcomes.
HIT sessions should be programmed considering the total weekly training load including other physical and technical sessions to avoid overload and minimize musculoskeletal injury risk.
Classical HIT formats include short intervals (e.g. 30s work/30s rest) and long intervals (2-4 min at high submaximal intensity), with work intervals ranging from less than 45 seconds to 2-4 minutes.
